title: US Delays Blacklisting DeepSeek, OpenAI and Google Found Supplying Blacklisted Chinese Firms, and G7 Floats AI Alliance Framework
subhead: The Trump administration is holding off on adding DeepSeek and 100+ flagged Chinese firms to the trade blacklist, even as OpenAI and Google are revealed to have sold AI services to Pentagon-blacklisted Chinese giants via Singapore subsidiaries — exposing a widening gap between US rhetoric on AI competition and the actual regulatory architecture.

sections.heading: US Holds Off Blacklisting DeepSeek and 100+ Chinese Firms
sections.body:
The Trump administration has delayed adding DeepSeek, CXMT, and more than 100 Chinese companies flagged as national security risks to the Commerce Department's trade blacklist, according to Reuters. The decision is driven by a desire to avoid further escalation with Beijing at a moment when diplomatic ties are fragile — Trump visited Beijing in May, and AI chips and critical minerals dominated those talks.

Washington has already limited sales of Nvidia's H200 chips to roughly 10 Chinese firms and blocked exports of its most advanced processors. But the broader question of whether to extend controls from hardware to software and services remains unresolved. The delay on DeepSeek — which demonstrated that frontier AI can be trained on chips that US export controls were designed to restrict — underscores the tension between two competing impulses: tightening the noose on Chinese AI development and preserving negotiating room with Beijing.
sections.whyItMatters: The blacklist delay signals that the US is prioritizing diplomatic space over maximum pressure on Chinese AI right now. But the longer DeepSeek and others operate without formal restrictions, the more the hardware-focused export control regime looks like it's regulating the wrong layer — chips are controlled, but model access and cloud services are not.

sections.heading: OpenAI and Google Sold AI Services to Pentagon-Blacklisted Chinese Firms via Singapore
sections.body:
Both OpenAI and Google have provided advanced AI model access to Singapore-based subsidiaries of Alibaba, Baidu, and Tencent, companies included on the Pentagon's 1260H list for alleged ties to China's military, the Financial Times reported on July 11. The transactions were legal under current US rules, which do not broadly bar Chinese-headquartered firms from using American AI services outside mainland China.

OpenAI confirmed it suspended API access for Alibaba-affiliated users last month after detecting suspected 'distillation' — using one model's outputs to train a rival system. Google said its AI services remain available in Singapore and Hong Kong, subject to rules against distillation, but acknowledged that geographic restrictions alone cannot stop determined users from bypassing them.

Anthropic has taken a stricter stance, barring Chinese companies and their overseas affiliates from using its frontier models entirely. Chris McGuire of the Council on Foreign Relations told the FT: 'The administration says we need to beat China on AI all the time, but the problem is they haven't done anything on export control, which is the actual tool we have to slow China down.'
sections.whyItMatters: This is the most concrete evidence yet that US AI export controls have a software-shaped hole. The government has built an elaborate semiconductor restriction regime, but AI model access — which can cross borders instantly — remains largely unregulated. The Singapore loophole shows that blacklist status means little when subsidiaries in third countries can buy the same services legally.

sections.heading: G7 Leaders Discuss Trusted Partner Access to US AI Models
sections.body:
At the G7 summit in Evian-les-Bains, leaders discussed a framework to grant select trusted partners access to advanced AI models from US firms such as Anthropic, three diplomatic sources told Reuters. The concept would create a tiered access system — opening a path for allied nations to use frontier AI capabilities while keeping them restricted from adversaries.

Japan's Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi separately pushed for a coordinated G7 response to China's use of export controls and trade leverage, signaling rising concern over what she framed as 'China Shock 2.0' — a new wave of cheap exports pressuring European and Japanese manufacturers.

Brazil's top foreign policy adviser Celso Amorim, speaking at a Rio security conference, framed the AI race as a two-power contest: 'Neither Latin America nor Europe is leading the artificial intelligence race. What we see today are two poles, the United States and China.' His prescription: draw capital and technology from both sides through BRICS rather than picking one.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 trusted-partner concept, if implemented, would be the first multilateral framework for AI model access — essentially a technology equivalent of NATO's dual-use export regimes. But it also highlights the contradiction: the US wants to restrict Chinese access while expanding allied access, which requires a regulatory structure for software that barely exists today.

sections.heading: China's AI Price War Deepens as Capability Gaps Narrow
sections.body:
A price war is accelerating across China's AI sector as companies slash rates amid converging model capabilities. ByteDance introduced Seedance 2.0 Mini at 23 yuan per million tokens — half the price of its standard version. Xiaomi cut its MiMo V2.5 model by 99 percent. Tencent slashed its Hy-MT2-Pro price by nearly 70 percent. MiniMax AI halved prices for its new M3 series. Alibaba offered 50 percent off Qwen3.7-Max during the 618 midyear sales.

Bank of America Securities analysts noted in a research note that 'limited capability gaps across incumbents' are driving the competitive pressure — the models are converging in quality, so price becomes the only meaningful differentiator.

In parallel, Hong Kong is building domestic AI infrastructure. Hong Kong Science and Technology Park partnered with SenseTime to build a home-grown AI data center targeting 40,000 petaflops by 2030. SenseTime CEO Xu Li described a 'train on the mainland, process in Hong Kong, serve the world' model — leveraging Hong Kong's financial and legal infrastructure to reach international clients.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapsing price of Chinese AI models changes the economics globally. If frontier-quality inference drops to near-zero in China, it undercuts the business case for expensive US model subscriptions in price-sensitive markets — particularly across the Global South where Amorim says countries are already sidelined. Hong Kong's data center play is an explicit attempt to bridge mainland training capacity with international market access.

sections.heading: Sovereign AI Race: Canada's Data Residency Illusion and the CLOUD Act Problem
sections.body:
AI inference startup DeepInfra opened Canada's first Blackwell-generation AI inference cluster in Toronto on July 8, deploying over 1,000 NVIDIA B300 GPUs with 1.7 MW of capacity. The facility addresses a real compliance friction point for Canadian enterprises subject to PIPEDA and Quebec's Law 25, which restrict cross-border data transfers.

But as TechTimes reported, the cluster exposes a deeper problem. DeepInfra is US-incorporated, meaning the CLOUD Act allows US law enforcement to compel data production from its servers regardless of where they physically sit. Data residency is not data sovereignty.

Canada's Prime Minister Mark Carney launched the 'AI for All' national strategy on June 4, explicitly describing current dependencies on foreign-controlled cloud infrastructure as 'a strategic exposure Canada cannot afford to leave in place.' The strategy targets 850 MW of sovereign compute capacity by 2030 — defined as Canadian-controlled infrastructure, a standard DeepInfra's Toronto cluster does not meet.

A SiliconANGLE analysis of the sovereign AI landscape found that more than 60 nations have published formal AI strategies and over 30 have committed specific domestic funding. The CNAS Sovereign AI Index tracks 130+ national initiatives, with over 80% of disclosed investment concentrated in the Middle East and East Asia. Saudi Arabia's HUMAIN alone is building 11 data centers at 200 MW each, with a stated ambition to become the world's third-largest AI provider.
sections.whyItMatters: The distinction between data residency and data sovereignty is about to become the central regulatory fight in AI infrastructure. DeepInfra's Toronto cluster is a case study: it solves a compliance problem while leaving the sovereignty gap wide open. Canada's explicit recognition that foreign-controlled compute is a strategic vulnerability is likely the first of many such declarations.

sections.heading: China's Low-Cost Stealth Coating and FCC Approves Orbital Mirror Satellite
sections.body:
Chinese researchers at Foshan University reported a low-cost microwave-absorbing composite made from expanded graphite and titanium dioxide, published in the peer-reviewed Journal of Ceramics in April. The material uses cheap, widely available ingredients instead of expensive graphene or carbon nanotubes, potentially enabling mass production of radar-absorbing coatings. SCMP reported that commercial stealth paint may already be available on the open market in China, which would drastically lower the barrier to radar-evading technology.

Separately, the FCC approved the first Reflect Orbital satellite on July 9, authorizing the launch of Eärendel-1 — a 142-kilogram spacecraft with an 18-meter thin-film reflector designed to direct sunlight to specific ground locations. Reflect Orbital has proposed operating up to 50,000 such satellites. The European Southern Observatory warned the full constellation would increase background sky brightness by a factor of three to four at its Chilean telescope facilities. The FCC concluded that astronomy and environmental concerns fell outside its jurisdiction.
sections.whyItMatters: China's cheap stealth material isn't a breakthrough in performance — it's a breakthrough in cost. If radar-absorbing coatings can be mass-produced at house-paint prices, it changes the economics of stealth from boutique military tech to widely accessible capability. The FCC's orbital mirror approval, meanwhile, establishes a precedent that commercial innovation interests outweigh astronomical and environmental concerns — a regulatory gap that will widen as the orbital economy grows.

sections.heading: Türkiye Angle: Sovereign AI Ambitions Meet Export Control Reality
sections.body:
While Türkiye has not been a focal point of this week's tech-geopolitics developments, the sovereign AI debate directly affects Ankara's positioning. Türkiye currently depends on US and European cloud providers for AI compute, placing it in the same strategic exposure category that Canada's strategy explicitly flags. The CLOUD Act problem applies equally to Turkish data hosted on US-incorporated platforms.

The G7's discussion of a 'trusted partner' AI access framework could eventually provide Türkiye with a formal pathway to advanced US AI models — but it would also deepen dependency on American technology. Meanwhile, China's price war in AI services creates an alternative: cheap, nearly-free inference from Chinese providers, with the security risks that entails.

India's approach — launching a sovereign LLM initiative and courting both Pacific partners and domestic compute — offers a potential model for middle-power nations seeking to avoid choosing between the two poles.
sections.whyItMatters: Türkiye's position mirrors that of many middle powers: too integrated with Western systems to easily pivot, too strategically autonomous to accept full dependency. The sovereign AI question — who controls the compute, who holds the data, who can pull the plug — will be answered in the next 2-3 years, and countries without domestic infrastructure plans will find the options narrowing fast.

bottomLine: The central tension this week is the growing gap between US AI export control rhetoric and the actual regulatory architecture. The government delays blacklisting DeepSeek while OpenAI and Google legally supply frontier AI to blacklisted Chinese firms through Singapore subsidiaries. The G7 proposes a trusted-partner access framework, but there is no software export control regime to enforce it. China's AI price war is collapsing inference costs — making the technology cheaper and more accessible globally, including to exactly the actors Washington wants to restrict. Meanwhile, the sovereign AI movement is accelerating: Canada declared foreign-controlled compute a strategic vulnerability, 130+ nations have launched domestic AI initiatives, and Saudi Arabia is building out 11 data centers targeting third-place globally. The control layer is shifting from chips to services, and the regulatory framework hasn't caught up.
sources: Reuters, Financial Times, SCMP, AP News, TechTimes, SiliconANGLE, IBTimes Singapore, SpaceNews, Defense News, Nikkei Asia, Yahoo Finance/Benzinga, Canada ISED, CNAS Sovereign AI Index
